The area marked in the image is the mantle zone of a lymph node.
The mantle zone is produced as surrounding cells are marginalised by the rapidly growing
germinal centre. It is populated by mainly quiescent B cells with condensed heterochromatic
nuclei and little cytoplasm (hence the deeply basophilic staining of this region in routine
preparations), a few helper T cells, Follicular Dendritic cells and macrophages.
In peripheral lymph nodes, the marginal zone are often inapparent because they are mixed with
the mantle zone cells. And often not well developed in lymph nodes.
The paracortex lies between the cortical follicles and the medulla and is populated mainly by T
cells, which are not organised into follicles

The given clinical scenario of hypoparathyroidism, absent thymus, and tetany is suggestive of
DiGeorge syndrome. It is caused by defective development of the third and fourth pharyngeal
pouch. In the image of pharyngeal pouches, "3" points to the thymus (derivative of 3rd pharyngeal
pouch). Since in the above question, the thymus is absent, option 3 will be the most appropriate
answer.
(oaratotteutar © cent)
DiGeorge syndrome is a complex embryopathy that is caused by the hemizygous interstitial
deletion of chromosome 22. It is characterized by multiple developmental defects, including
conotruncal defects, hypoparathyroidism, and dysmorphie syndrome. It is also known as
velocardiofacial syndrome (palate, facial, and cardiac defects).
It has variable presentations including Cleft palate, Abnormal facies, Thymic aplasia leading to
T-cell deficiency, Cardiac defects, and Hypocalcemia secondary to parathyroid
aplasia (CATCH-22).

subject: Anatomy
question: Inferior thyroid artery is the branch of which of the following?

explanation.detail:
Inferior thyroid artery is a branch of thyrocervical trunk, which in turn, is a branch of the first
part of the subclavian artery. The inferior thyroid artery loops upwards anterior to the medial
border of the scalenus anterior, turns medially just below the sixth cervical transverse process,
and then descends on longus colli to the lower border of the thyroid gland.
Relations between the terminal branches and recurrent laryngeal nerve are very variable and of
considerable surgical importance.

subject: Anatomy
question: A patient presents to you with inability to form words but can make noises. He also knows what he wants to say. What area of the brain has been damaged in this patient?

explanation.detail:
The given clinical scenario is suggestive of Broca's aphasia in which the Broca's area will be
affected.
In Broca’s aphasia/ Motor aphasia / Non-fluent aphasia, areas 44 and 45 in the prefrontal and
premotor facial regions of the cerebral cortex are affected. The affected individuals will
have difficulty forming verbal or written words. The comprehension aspect of speech
is preserved but the person cannot repeat or name the words and lacks fluency.
Patients in whom the area is severely affected may have very few words to express all of their
emotions.

subject: Anatomy
question: Which of the following nerves 1s not involved in olfaction’?

explanation.detail:
The hypoglossal nerve is purely motor cranial nerve and is not involved in olfaction.
The other three cranial nerves—trigeminal, glossopharyngeal, and vagus nerves are mixed cranial
nerves and contribute to olfaction.
Vagal nerve stimulation modifies the electrical activity of the olfactory bulb.

Ligamentum flavum has high levels of elastin fibres.
The ligamenta flava are a series of ligaments that connect the ventral parts of the laminae of
adjacent vertebrae. They predominantly contain elastic tissue and form part of the posterior
surface of the vertebral canal. The high content of elastin allows it to be stretched by 80% without
damage. The ligamentum flava resist separation of the laminae in flexion and assist in extension
back to the anatomical position.

question: Which muscle is not involved in the inversion of foot?

explanation.detail:
Peroneus longus muscle is not involved in the inversion of the foot. It causes eversion and plantar
flexion.
Inversion of the foot is a movement in which the medial border of the foot is elevated so that the
sole faces medially.
+ Tibialis anterior - Causes dorsiflexion and inversion of the foot
+ Tibialis posterior - Causes plantar flexion and inversion of the foot
Eversion of the foot is a movement where the lateral border of the foot is elevated so that the sole
faces laterally. Peroneus longus, peroneus brevis, and peroneus tertius are the muscles involved.
These movements take place, mainly at the talocalcaneal and the talocalcaneonavicular joints, and
partly at the transverse tarsal joint.

Meissner corpuscles are present in the papillary dermis. These corpuscles are rapidly adapting
mechanoreceptors particularly sensitive to the movement of objects over the skin's surface and
low-frequency vibrations. Their acute sensitivity plays a crucial role in tasks such as reading
Braille text.
Itis an elongated encapsulated nerve ending of a large myelinated sensory nerve fiber (type
Ap). Each corpuscle consists of a connective tissue capsule and a central core composed of stacked
modified Schwann cells. Meissner's corpuscles are found in nonhairy areas of the skin, especially
in fingertips, lips, and other regions where spatial touch perception is highly developed.

trapezoid. This can be mapped out using the percussion method by looking for cardiae dullness.

+ Upper border: Slopes gently from the second left costal cartilage to the third right costal
cartilage. It corresponds to the left atrium.
+ Right border: Convex to the right and runs from the third to the sixth right costal cartilages, 1-2
cm lateral to the sternal edge. It corresponds to the right atrium.
+ Inferior border or acute border: Runs leftwards from the 6th right costal cartilage to the cardiac
apex which is located approximately 9 em lateral to the midline, often in the left 5th intercostal
space or level with the 5th or 6th rib. It corresponds to the right ventricle.
+ Left border or obtuse border: Convex laterally on the left. It extends superomedially from the
cardiac apex to meet the second left costal cartilage about 1 cm from the left sternal edge. It
corresponds to the left ventricle.

subject: Anatomy
question: Where are the Ito cells in the liver located?

explanation.detail:
Ito cells/Hepatie stellate cells are located in the space of Disse. They are mesenchymal in origin.
The space of Disse or the perisinusoidal space (or space of Disse) is a location in the liver
between hepatocytes and the liver sinusoid.
Significance of Ito cells:
Ito cells are in a dormant state for the majority of the duration. They store the fat-soluble vitamin
Ain their lipid droplets and are a signiffeant source of growth factors involved in liver
homeostasis and regeneration.
When there is liver injury, Ito cells get activated. They replace damaged hepatocytes with
collagenous scar tissue and secrete intralobular matrix components, including collagen type III
(reticular) mbres. This leads to hepatic mbrosis.
Note: A narrow space which separates limiting plate (a sleeve of modified hepatocytes) from the
connective tissue of the portal area is known as space of moll.

Absence of striated duct in exocrine pancreas differentiates it from the parotid gland.
The exocrine pancreas is comprised entirely of serous acini, Hence, its general structure is similar

+ The parotid gland has striated and intercalated intralobular ducts, whereas the pancreas has
only the intercalated type.
+ The excretory duets of the parotid gland are striated columnar, whereas the excretory duct of the
pancreas is a simple columnar.

question: Medial lemniscus is a continuation of which of the following structures?

explanation.detail:
Medial lemniscus is a continuation of fasciculus gracilis.
Posterior column-medial lemniscal pathway:
First-order axons carrying discriminative touch and vibration sensations enter the dorsal
horn and continue traveling upward in the posterior white column as the fasciculus gracilis
and fasciculus cuneatus.
Fibers of these fasciculi synapse on the second-order neurons of the ipsilateral nuclei gracilis and
cuneatus in the medulla oblongata, respectively.
Axons of these second-order neurons then cross over as the internal areuate fibers to form the
medial lemniscus in the contralateral medulla.
These second-order axons ascend through the brainstem to reach the third-order neuronal cell
bodies in the ventral posterior lateral nucleus of the thalamus. Axons from the third-order
neurons then project through the posterior limb of the internal capsule to reach the primary

explanation.detail:
The fibers present in the fornix are association fibers except for the hippocampal commissure of
fornix which has commissural fibers. Hence the most appropriate answer is option A. i.e both
association and commissural fibers.
Association fibers connect various cortical regions within the same hemisphere whereas
commissural fibers connect corresponding regions of two hemispheres
Fornix arises from the hippocampus and ends in the mamillary body of the hypothalamus. It is

Note: The function of the commissure of the fornix is to connect the hippocampal formations
of the two sides.

sternocleidomastoid. It is enclosed by the investing layer of deep cervical fascia.
The deep cervical fascia of the neck is organized into the following layers:
+ The investing layer, which surrounds all structures in the neck
+ The prevertebral layer, which surrounds the vertebral column and the deep muscles associated
with the back.
+ The pretracheal layer, which encloses the viscera of the neck

In the neck, the pretracheal fascia covers the strap muscles i.e sternohyoid, omohyoid,
sternothyroid, thyrohyoid. It splits to enclose the thyroid gland. It also surrounds the trachea and
the esophagus including the recurrent laryngeal nerve present in the groove between them.

Paradoxical movements in the chest are as follows:
+ Paradoxical respiration - the abnormal movement of the chest wall or abdominal wall, where
they move in during inspiration instead of moving out. It is seen in flail chest.
+ Paradoxical movement of the diaphragm - normally, the diaphragm moves down during
inspiration. However, in cases of phrenic nerve palsy, the affected side of the diaphragm moves
upward (paradoxical respiration) due to diaphragmatic paralysis. The gold standard investigation
to detect this is the electrical or magnetic stimulation of the phrenic nerve with a recording of the
compound muscle action potential and/or the transdiaphragmatic pressure. This can be observed
on ultrasound or fluoroscopy.

subject: Anatomy
question: Reticular fibres are not present in which of the following structures?

explanation.detail:
Reticular fibres are not present in the thymus.
Reticular fibres are fine, anastomosing fibres composed of mainly type III collagen. They differ
from type I collagen fibres in that, they are finer and are arranged in the form of a network
(reticulum). The fibres appear black in colour on staining with silver impregnation. Due to this
affinity to silver salts, they are also known as argentophil fibres.
Reticular fibres - silver impregnation method
Reticular fibres are mainly found in:
+ All lymphoid organs like spleen, lymph node, and bone marrow (except thymus).
+ Glands, liver and kidneys.
+ The basement membrane of all epithelia.

subject: Anatomy
question: The middle radioulnar joint is an example of a:

explanation.detail:
The middle radioulnar joint is an example of a fibrous joint (syndesmosis).
The radius and ulna are united with each other by three joints:
+ Proximal (superior) radioulnar joint
+ Middle radioulnar joint
+ Distal (inferior) radioulnar joint
Proximal and distal radioulnar joints are uniaxial pivot joints (synovial) and are involved in
pronation and supination movements of the forearm.
The middle radioulnar joint is a fibrous joint (syndesmosis). The radius and ulna, in the middle,
are connected by two syndesmoses namely:
+ The oblique cord - extends from the lateral side of the ulnar tuberosity to the radius, distal to the
radial tuberosity.
+ Interosseous membrane - a broad, thin collagenous structure, which runs perpendicular to the
oblique cord. It extends distomedially between the interosseous borders of the radius and ulna.
Based on the structure, joints are classified into three types:
+ Fibrous joints
+ Sutures
+ Syndesmosis
+ Gomphosis
+ Cartilaginous joints
+ Primary cartilaginous joints or synchondrosis
+ Secondary cartilaginous joints or symphysis
+ Synovial joints
+ Ball-and-socket or spheroidal joints
+ Sellar or saddle joints
+ Condylar or bicondylar joints
+ Ellipsoid joints
+ Hinge joints
+ Pivot or trochoid joints
Types of fibrous joints

gland
+ Posterior branch - supplies the skin over the mastoid process, posteroinferior part of the auricle,
lobule, and concha.

sternocleidomastoid muscle. (as seen in the above image)

auricular nerve causes gustatory sweating,

question: All of the following are true about the right coronary artery except
options: It's diameter is less than left coronary artery
It arises from the anterior aortic sinus
It gives rise to circumflex coronary branch
Right conal artery is it's first branch

explanation.detail:
The right coronary artery does not give rise to the circumflex coronary artery.
The right coronary artery arises from the anterior aortic sinus (right coronary sinus) of the
ascending aorta. It passes anteriorly and descends vertically in the coronary sulcus between the
right atrium and the right ventricle. On reaching the inferior margin, it turns posteriorly and
continues in the sulcus onto the diaphragmatic surface and the base of the heart.
The diameter of the right coronary artery is less than that of the left coronary artery.
The right conal artery is, usually the first branch of the right coronary artery.
The circumflex coronary artery is a branch of the left coronary artery and is the exclusive supplier
to the right bundle branch.

question: Foot drop is caused by damage to:

explanation.detail:
Foot drop is caused by damage to the common peroneal nerve.
The common peroneal nerve is a component of the sciatic nerve. In the posterior thigh, it supplies
the short head of the biceps femoris. It curves around the neck of the fibula deep to peroneus
longus and divides into superficial and deep peroneal nerves.

dorsiflexors of the foot, extensor digitorum brevis, first two dorsal interossei muscles, and skin
between great and second toes.
Hence, injury to the common peroneal nerve will lead to:
+ Weakness of dorsiflexion of foot or foot drop
+ Loss of eversion
+ Loss of extension of toes
+ Sensory loss
+ Anterior and lateral side of the leg
+ Dorsum of the foot including the medial side of the great toe

Characteristics of Purkinje cells:
+ Purkinje cells are the flask-shaped cells present in the Purkinje cell layer of the cerebellum,
+ The cells are evenly spaced and are arranged in a single layer.
+ A dendrite arises from the neck of each flask-shaped cell, which passes upward to enter the
molecular layer, which then branches extensively to form a dendritic tree.
+ The axon of each cell passes downwards, crosses the granular cell layer to enter the white
matter.
+ Axons of Purkinje cells are the only efferents of the cerebellar cortex.
+ The initial segment of the axon receives synaptic contacts from basket cell axons
+ The main axon terminates in one of the cerebellar or vestibular nuclei.
+ Purkinje cells are inhibitory and use GABA as their neurotransmitter.
Pathological significance:
+ Negri bodies in rabies, are usually found as eosinophilic, cytoplasmic inclusions in the neurons
of the hippocampus and Purkinje cells
+ Loss of Pur'
je cells and granule cells is seen in ataxia-telangiectasi:
+ In patients with subacute cerebellar degeneration, there is the destruction of Purkinj
Some patients show circulating PCA1 antibody (antiYo) against Purkinje cells.
+ Purkinje cells and neurons in pyramidal cell layers of hippocampus and cerebral cortex are the
most sensitive neurons to hypoxia, and are likely to be damaged in cases of global cerebral
ischemia.

subject: Anatomy
question: The long left recurrent laryngeal nerve is due to the persistence of which arch artery?

explanation.detail:
The long left recurrent laryngeal nerve is due to the persistence of the left 6th arch artery.
During the development, the laryngeal nerves initially arise below the 6th arch artery and cross
here to innervate the intrinsic muscles of the larynx, As the larynx moves cranially relative to the
aortic arch arteries, the recurrent laryngeal nerves on the right and left sides are caught under the
6th arch arteries.
On the right side, the distal 6th arch artery disappears. Since there is no sth arch artery, the right
recurrent laryngeal nerve is caught under the 4th arch artery which becomes the future
right subclavian artery.
On the left side, the 6th arch artery persists, which develops into ductus arteriosus, which later
forms ligamentum arteriosum. Due to the persistence of the 6th arch artery, the left recurrent
laryngeal nerve is caught under it and therefore, has a relatively long course.

question: A patient presented with weakness of the right side of the face with the loss of pain and temperature. Pain and temperature sensation of the left leg is also lost. The lesion is most likely located at?

explanation.detail:
The above clinical scenario is suggestive of lateral pontine syndrome which occurs due to a lesion

Lateral pontine syndrome occurs due to the involvement of the anterior inferior cerebellar artery.
+ Ipsilateral manifestations include:
+ CN VIL: Facial paralysis
+ CN V: loss of pain and temperature sensation over the face.
+ Vestibular nerve or nucleus: Horizontal and vertical nystagmus, vertigo, nausea, vomiting,
oscillopsia
+ Auditory nerve or cochlear nucleus: Deafness, tinnitus.
+ Middle cerebellar peduncle: Ataxia of limb and gait.
+ Contralateral manifestations include:
+ Spinothalamic tract: Loss of pain and temperature sensation over the body.
Medial pontine syndrome occurs due to the involvement of the paramedian branch of the basilar
artery.
+ Ipsilateral manifestation
+ CN VI: Diplopia(lateral rectus muscle paralysis - the affected eye looks downwards and inwards)
+ Vestibular nucleus: Nystagmus.
+ Middle cerebellar peduncle: Ataxia of limb and gait.
+ Contralateral manifestation
+ Corticobulbar and corticospinal tract: Paralysis of face, arm, and leg.

Fontanelle tap/ Percutaneous ventricular puncture:
A fontanelle tap or percutaneous ventricular puncture is performed in infants without access to a
VP shunt, who require urgent ventricular decompression and have an open anterior fontanelle.
The infants’ open fontanelle is directly punctured with a butterfly needle to enter the lateral
ventricle to collect CSF samples or release the pressure or place a drain/eatheter.
A 25-gauge butterfly needle is inserted through the anterolateral aspect of the anterior fontanelle
(away from the midline, avoiding the superior sagittal sinus).

The scalp is a soft tissue that covers the calvaria of the skull. It consists of five layers (Mnemonic:

reach the subarachnoid space below the arachnoid layer to collect or tap CSF. So, the layers
pierced in the order are as follows:
+ Skin
+ Connective tissue (superficial fascia) with blood vessels and nerves
+ Aponeurosis (galea aponeurotica) with occipitofrontalis muscle
+ Loose areolar (subaponeurotic) tissue
+ Pericranium:
+ Dura mater
+ Arachnoid membrane

Types of Hemorrhage seen during/post tonsillectomy:
+ Primary hemorthage:
+ Occurs during surgery due to injury to blood vessels.
+ Can be controlled by pressure, ligation, or electrocoagulation of the bleeding vessels.
+ Reactionary hemorrhage
+ It occurs within 24 hours of surgery.

+ Secondary hemorrhage:
+ It occurs between 24 hours to 10 days (most common between sth to 8th postoperative days).
+ It is due to sepsis and premature separation of the membrane.
+ The patient is started on IV antibiotics and if not controlled, general anesthesia is given and the
bleeding vessel is electrocoagulated or ligated.

The muscle marked in the given CT image is that of the medial rectus. The medial rectus is
supplied by the oculomotor nerve, the nucleus of which is situated at the level of the superior
colliculus close to the red nucleus.
The nuclear complex from which the oculomotor nerve arises consists of:
+ Several groups of large motor neurons are collectively called the oculomotor nucleus. The motor
neuron groups of the oculomotor nucleus innervate, in dorsoventral order, the ipsilateral inferior
rectus, inferior oblique, and medial rectus. A medially placed column of cells innervates
the contralateral superior rectus.
+ Smaller preganglionic parasympathetic neurons called the Edinger—Westphal preganglionic
nucleus
Red Nucleus/ Nucleus Ruber
It is a rounded mass of grey matter located dorsomedial to the substantial nigra. It has a reddish
hue in fresh specimens due to its extensive vascularity and the presence of ferric iron pigments in
multipolar neurons.
Clinical correlate:

The peduncle highlighted in the image is the superior cerebellar peduncle. The superior cerebellar
peduncle includes fibers from deep cerebellar nuclei that project to the brainstem, red nucleus,

The superior cerebellar peduncle contains all the efferent fibers from the dentate, emboliform,

The given clinical vignette is suggestive of injury to axillary nerve which leads to deltoid muscle

The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid muscle. Iatrogenic injuries of the axillary nerve can occur

Injury to the axillary nerve can lead to:
+ Weakness of abduction and lateral rotation of the arm
+ Retropulsion (extension of the shoulder with the arm abducted to 90 degrees) is impossible.
+ Atrophy of the Deltoid muscle leading to the loss of muscle contours of the shoulder
+ Loss of sensation in the Regimental badge area

The structure involved in causing lurching gait in the child is gluteus medius (marked as 2).
The gluteus medius originates from the outer surface of the ilium between the anterior and
posterior gluteal lines. Insertion of the muscle is at the lateral surface of the greater trochanter. It
is supplied by the superior gluteal nerve.
Gluteus medius and minimus are powerful abductors of the hip joint. Mechanism of actions of
gluteus medius and minimus:
+ When the leg on one side is lifted off the ground, the gluteus medius and minimus muscles, on
the opposite supported side, exert traction on the hip bone by acting from the femur. This action
results in a slight raising of the pelvis on the unsupported side, which helps maintain the trunk in
the upright position.
+ The actions of these muscles are affected when there is an injury to the superior gluteal nerve,
neck of the femur, or loss of normal angulation of the shaft of the femur. This occurs in conditions
like paralysis of the glutei, developmental dysplasia of the hip, etc.
+ When the gluteus medius and minimus are paralyzed, the pelvis sinks down on the unsupported
(normal) side, when the patient tries to stand on the affected limb. This leads to a positive
Trendelenburg's sign with a characteristic lurching gait - the patient lurches towards the affected
side with contralateral pelvis drop.
Other options
1 - Gluteus maximus - involved in the extension of flexed thigh, abduction (only upper fibres), and
lateral rotation at the hip joint. Insertion is at the iliotibial tract.
3 - Piriformis - involved in lateral rotation and assists abduction at the hip joint. It is inserted into
the medial side of greater trochanter of the femur. Entrapment of the sciatic nerve in the
piriformis can lead to piriformis syndrome.
4 Sciatic nerve - The nerve passes over the obturator internus, gemellus superior, gemellus
inferior and quadratus femoris. It supplies knee flexors and muscles below the knee. Paralysis of
this nerve results in flail foot and severe difficulty in walking.

The structure marked in the image is a flask-shaped neuron in the cerebellar cortex, which is
a Purkinje cell. It is inhibitory to deep cerebellar nuclei.
Purkinje cells are the flask-shaped cells present in the Purkinje cell layer of the cerebellum. They
are evenly spaced and arranged in a single layer between molecular and granular cell layers.
The dendrite from each cell passes upward to enter the molecular layer and the axon of each cell
passes downwards, crosses the granular cell layer, and terminates on the deep cerebellar
nuclei, They are inhibitory and use GABA as their neurotransmitter.
Axons of Purkinje cells are the only efferents of the cerebellar cortex. They receive excitatory input
from the climbing fibers from the inferior olivary nucleus and mossy fibers.
Pathological significance of Purkinje cells:
+ Negri bodies in rabies are usually found as eosinophilic, cytoplasmic inclusions in the neurons of
the hippocampus and Purkinje cells of the cerebellum.
+ In paraneoplastic cerebellar degeneration, there is the destruction of Purkinje cells due to
antibodies directed against them
+ Purkinje cells and neurons in pyramidal cell layers of the hippocampus and cerebral cortex are
the most sensitive neurons to hypoxia and are likely to be damaged in cases of global cerebral
ischemia.

The structure marked in the given image is the azygos vein, which develops from the
supracardinal vein.
The azygos system of veins forms a connecting channel between the superior and inferior venae
cavae. It occupies the upper part of the posterior abdominal wall and the posterior mediastinum.
It also connects the portal, caval system, and vertebral venous systems.It drains the thoracic wall
and the upper lumbar region.
Development: The azygos venous system arises from the supra cardinal veins embryologically.
The right supra cardinal vein becomes the azygos vein, while the left supra cardinal vein becomes
with a portion of the posterior cardinal vein forms the azygos vein.
+ On the left, the 4th to 7th intercostal veins enter into the left supracardinal vein, and the left
supracardinal vein, then known as the hemiazygos vein, which also empties into the azygos vein.
Other options: Cardinal veins form the main venous drainage system of the embryo. It consists of,
+ Anterior cardinal veins, which drain the cephalic part of the embryo, and
+ Posterior cardinal veins, which drain the rest of the embryo.
During the fifth to the seventh weeks, a number of additional veins are formed. It includes,
+ Subcardinal veins, which mainly drain the kidney
+ Sacrocardinal veins, which drain the lower extremities;
+ Supracardinal veins, which drain the body wall by way of the intercostal veins.

Pelvic diaphragm
The muscular part of the pelvic floor is the pelvic diaphragm. It is attached to the pelvic wall on
either side between the greater and lesser sciatic foramen. Levator ani and coccygeus muscles
form the pelvic diaphragm and delineate the lower limit of the true pelvis.
Levator ani is formed by pubococcygeus, iliococcygeus, and puborectalis. The muscles blend
together posterior to the vagina in women and around the anus in both sexes. Posterior to the
anal aperture, the form the anococeygeal body attaches to the coccyx. Urogenital hiatus is present
anteriorly separating the muscles. This allows the urethra and vagina to pass through. Levator ani
reinforces the external anal sphincter, In women, it functions as the vaginal sphincter.
The coceygeus muscles, one on each side are triangular in shape and form the posterior part of the
pelvic diaphragm. The apices are attached to the tips of ischial spines. The bases are attached to
the coccyx and sacrum.

question: A patient presented with vision loss. On radiological investigation, an aneurysm causing damage to the optic chiasma was noted. Which of the following arteries is most likely to be the artery that is causing the damage?

explanation.detail:
An aneurysm of the anterior communicating artery is most likely to be causing damage to the
optic chiasma, leading to vision loss in this patient.
Circle of Willis is a large arterial anastomosis that unites the internal carotid and vertebrobasilar
systems. It lies in the subarachnoid space within the basal cisterns that surround the optic
chiasma and infundibulum.
Anterior communicating artery aneurysm:
Anterior communicating artery (Acom) links the right and left anterior cerebral arteries and is
directed anterior and superior to the optic chiasma. The aneurysm will protrude in the posterior
direction and will impinge upon the optic chiasma from above, leading to loss of vision.
Compression of the optic chiasma (central lesion) by an ACom aneurysm, will lead to bitemporal
hemianopia i.e, loss of vision in the left/temporal halves of both visual fields. Nasal fibers of each
retina cross through the chiasma at this level and hence, these fibers will be compromised
In the case of a bilateral peripheral lesion of the optic chiasma, binasal hemianopia will be seen.
Other causes of chiasmal lesions are
+ Pituitary adenoma (most common)
+ Craniopharyngioma
+ Meningioma
+ Glioma
Blood supply of optic chiasma: The left and right anterior cerebral arteries, along with the
superior hypophyseal artery (branch of the internal carotid artery) form the blood supply of optic
Blood supply Sf optic chiaam
Carotid artery
The given cadaveric image below is of the vascular structures (Circle of Willis) at the base of the
brain.
Investigation: Digital subtraction angiogram is the definitive imaging modality for aneurysms
arising from the circle of Willis. Other investigations are CT and MRI.
Other optioi
Aneurysms of the anterior choroidal artery (option B) and middle cerebral artery (option
©) aneurysms will not compress the optic chiasma as they are anatomically not in close proximity
to be able to exert pressure on the chiasma and cause loss of vision.
Despite the anatomical proximity of the anterior cerebral artery (option D) to the optic chiasma,
anterior communicating artery (Acom) aneurysm is the commonest intracranial aneurysm.

The spleen becomes palpable when it is enlarged 2-3 times more than its normal size. A
characteristic splenic notch can be felt in the medial border in an enlarged spleen. Also, fingers
cannot be insinuated between the left costal margin and the enlarged spleen.
In the classical method of palpation, the right hand (with fingers pointed towards the left costal
margin) is placed on the right iliac fossa. Palpation begins from the right iliac fossa towards the
left hypochondrium in an upwards and lateral direction. Between each breath of the patient, the
edge of the spleen can be felt.
In the hooking method, the hooked fingers of the right hand are placed under the left costal
margin, and with the left hand, pressure is exerted over the posterolateral aspect of the lower
thorax. Spleen is felt at the deep inspiratory phase.

The given histological image is a section of the tongue showing circumvallate papillae, the largest
papillae of the tongue.
Circumvallate papillae are large cylindrical structures that are around 8-12 in number, covered by
non-keratinized stratified squamous epithelium.
They are arranged in a V-shaped row immediately in front of the sulcus terminalis (not on the
sides of the tongue) on the dorsal surface of the tongue. It is narrower at its base than its apex.
Numerous taste buds (around 250 taste buds for each of the 8-12 papillae) are present within the
papillary walls and they contain chemosensory cells that synapse with the gustatory nerves.
Serous glands of von Ebner empty via the ducts into the base of the sulcus.
Identification: They can be identified by their characteristic dome-shaped structure lined by
stratified squamous epithelium.

on the dorsal part of the anterior 2/3rd of the tongue. All except filiform papillae contain taste
buds. They are covered by keratinized stratified squamous epithelium. It is supplied by the lingual
nerve.
Option D - Foliate papillae are red, leaf-like mucosal ridges that are present bilaterally in two
zones at the sides of the tongue near the sulcus terminalis. They are covered by non-keratinized
stratified squamous epithelium.

question: A 7-year-old boy was brought to the hospital with multiple fractures of the humerus secondary to a fall from height. On examination, there is difficulty in flexion of the elbow and supination of the forearm, and associated loss of sensation over the lateral aspect of the forearm. Which is the nerve most likely to be injured?

explanation.detail:
The given clinical vignette in a patient with difficulty in flexion of the elbow and supination of the
forearm, with associated loss of sensation over the lateral aspect of the forearm, is suggestive of
injury to the musculocutaneous nerve.
Fall from a height on an outstretched hand is one of the main causes of humerus fractures.
Course:
The musculocutaneous nerve (C5, C6, C7) is a branch of the lateral cord of the brachial plexus. It
emerges at the lower border of the pectoralis minor and pierces coracobrachialis. It then descends
between biceps brachii and brachialis where it supplies a branch to the shaft of the humerus. Then
it enters the elbow and continues as the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm,
It innervates all the muscles of the anterior compartment of the arm i.e.,
+ Biceps brachii - involved in flexion of elbow and supination of the forearm
+ Brachialis - involved in the flexion of the elbow
+ Coracobrachialis - flexes the arm at the glenchumeral joint
Hence, injury to the musculocutaneous nerve results in weakness of supination of forearm (biceps
brachii), weakness in flexion of the elbow (biceps brachii, brachialis), and loss of sensation over
the lateral aspect of the forearm (lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm).

question: A patient underwent a coronary bypass graft with the great saphenous vein. The patient now complains of paresthesia and sensory loss over the medial aspect of the leg and foot. Which of the following nerves is most likely to be involved?

explanation.detail:
The given clinical vignette in a patient with paresthesia and sensory loss over the medial aspect of
the leg and foot is suggestive of injury to the saphenous nerve.
Saphenous nerve:
Itis the longest and largest cutaneous branch of the femoral nerve and is the longest nerve in the
body. It arises from the posterior division of the femoral nerve. It then enters the adductor canal
and exits it by piercing the fascia lata between the gracilis and sartorius and supplies the skin over
the patella. The saphenous nerve then descends along the medial border of the tibia along with
the great saphenous vein (anterior to the vein) and supplies the skin on the medial side of
the knee, leg, and foot as far as up to the first metatarsophalangeal joint.
Saphenous nerve injury:
When the great saphenous vein is being harvested for coronary bypass graft, injury to the
saphenous nerve is common as it closely travels with the great saphenous vein. Sensory loss over
the medial side of the knee, leg, and foot is observed in saphenous nerve injury.
Saphenous
and the dorsum of the foot.
Option C: Femoral nerve gives rise to anterior cutaneous branches, the saphenous nerve, and
motor supply to the quadriceps and sartorius. Anterior cutaneous branches supply the skin on the
anterior thigh and knee. If the saphenous nerve was not an option in the question, the femoral
nerve would have been the next best answer.
Option D: Sural nerve injury causes sensory loss over the posterior and lateral aspects of the distal
third of the leg and lateral side of the foot and fifth toe. It is often used as an autologous
peripheral nerve graft as it is readily identified and easy to harvest.

The structure marked in the given sagittal section of the head and neck is the uvula. The sensory
supply to the uvula is by the glossopharyngeal nerve and it is tested in this case.
Nerve supply by glossopharyngeal nerve:
+ Sensory fibers to the uvula, tympanic cavity, pharyngotympanic tube, tonsils,
fauces, nasopharynx, and posterior third of the tongue.
+ Motor fibers to the stylopharyngeus muscle
+ Parasympathetic secretomotor fibers to the parotid gland
+ Gustatory fibers for the postsuleal part of the tongue.
Pharyngeal plexus:
The nerve supply of the pharynx is mainly from the pharyngeal plexus of nerves. It is formed by
the pharyngeal branches of the glossopharyngeal nerve and vagus nerve. It also has contributions
from the superior cervical ganglion. The vagus nerve provides the motor supply and the
glossopharyngeal nerve provides the sensory supply.
Nerve supply by the pharyngeal branch of the vagus nerve:
+ All the muscles of the soft palate(except tensor veli palatini - supplied by mandibular division of
the trigeminal nerve)
+ All the muscles of the pharynx(except stylopharyngeus - supplied by the glossopharyngeal nerve)
Note:
Previously cranial root of the spinal accessory nerve was thought to contribute to the motor
functions of the pharyngeal plexus, but recent evidence suggests that it is not a major part of the
motor drive to the pharyngeal plexus.
Innervation of the soft palate:
General sensation - It is mostly carried by the lesser palatine nerve which is a branch of maxillary
nerve. The sensation from posterior part is carried by the pharyngeal branches of the
glossopharyngeal nerve and from the plexus around the tonsil which is formed by the tonsillar
branches of the glossopharyngeal and lesser palatine nerves.
Secretomotor supply - It is by the lesser palatine nerve via postganglionic fibres from the
pterygopalatine ganglion.
Motor supply - It is by the pharyngeal branch of the vagus nerve except for tensor veli palatini
which is supplied by mandibular division of the trigeminal nerve.

subject: Anatomy
question: What is the volume of the prostate in normal adult males?

explanation.detail:
The volume of the prostate in normal adult males is 20cc.
Prostate is an accessory glandular tissue embedded in the fibromuscular stroma. It usually weighs
8 grams in the young adults and its weight ranges from 40-150 grams in men aged >50 years.
The prostate gland is situated in the lesser pelvis and surrounds the prostatic urethra which
extends from bladder base to the membranous urethra. Pubic symphysis and pubie arch lie
anteriorly to the prostate whereas rectourethralis and rectal ampulla lie posteriorly to it.
The prostate has three distinct zones as follows:
Transitional zon | Urethra Most common site for benign
e prostatic hyperplasia (BPH)
Central zone _| Ejaculatory ducts Atrophies secondary to BPH
Peripheral zone | Central and transitional zone | Most common site for prosta
s Pre-prostatic urethra tie carcinoma
The prostate is composed of 5 lobes. They are anterior, posterior, two lateral and one medial or
middle lobe.
The median lobe of the prostate forms the uvula vesicae. It is an elevation found in the lower part
of the bladder trigone. It is the most common site of adenoma.
Mercier's bar and Bell's bar
MARROW
Microstructure of the prostate gland:
The glandular tissue of the prostate gland is composed of many follicles with frequent internal
papillae. The follicular epithelium is either a single-layered or pseudostratified columnar
epithelum. These follicles open into canals, which join to form the main ducts. Small eosinophilic
colloid amyloid bodies called corpora amylacea are frequently seen within a follicle.
The prostatic ducts open into the prostatic sinus in the prostatic urethra. The duets of the prostate
follicles are lined by bilayered epithelium (basal cuboidal and apical columnar).
The secretions of the prostate are acidic and contain acid phosphatase, amylase, prostate-specifie
antigen (PSA), fibrin, and zine.

question: Which is not a branch of facial artery in the face?

explanation.detail:
Mental is not a branch of the facial artery but the branch of the maxillary artery in the face.
The first part of the maxillary artery gives rise to the mental artery as a terminal branch of the
inferior alveolar artery.
The facial artery is a branch of the external carotid artery which arises in the carotid triangle,
above the origin of the lingual artery. It runs upwards in the neck and curves around the
anteroinferior border of the masseter to enter the face.
The premasseteric artery passes along the anterior border of the masseter and the surrounding
tissues are supplied by it. The inferior labial artery mainly supplies the inferior labial glands. It
then anastomoses with its controlateral fellow and with the mental branch of the inferior alveolar
artery. The superior labial artery supplies the upper lip whereas the lateral nasal artery supplies
the dorsum and alae of the nose. Both these arteries anastomose with its controlateral fellow.

subject: Anatomy
question: Superior gluteal nerve supplies all of the following structures except:

explanation.detail:
The dorsal branches of the L4, L5, and S1 rami give rise to the superior gluteal nerve. It is
accompanied by superior gluteal vessels. This nerve leaves the pelvis via the greater sciatic
foramen above piriformis and divides into superior and inferior branches. It supplies gluteus
medius, gluteus minimus, and tensor fasciae latae.
The dorsal branches of the S1 and S2 rami give rise to the nerve to the piriformis.
Gluteus medius and gluteus minimus act as the abductors of the hip joint. Stabilizing the pelvis on
the femur during walking is done by these muscles. It is demonstrated by asking the subject to
stand on one limb. If the ipsilateral abductors are normal, the pelvis remains in a horizontal
position. If the ipsilateral abductors are weak, then the pelvis tilts towards the opposite
(unsupported) side. This is called positive Trendelenburg's sign.

subject: Anatomy
question: The rupture of penile urethra does not cause extravasation of blood into which of the following spaces?

explanation.detail:
Rupture of the penile urethra causes extravasation of blood into the superficial perineal pouch,
scrotum, penis, and then up to the lower anterior abdominal wall.
The superficial perineal pouch is a compartment of the perineum enclosed by the membranous
layer of the perineal fascia that surrounds the scrotum or labia and penis or clitoris.
Anteriorly, the membranous layer of perineal fascia is continuous over the pubic symphysis and
pubic bones. It is later continuous with the membranous layer of the anterior abdominal wall.
Thus, the fluid that accumulates in the perineal pouch extends to the lower anterior abdomen.
In the lower lateral abdominal wall, the abdominal fascia is attached to the deep fascia of the
thigh, inferior to the inguinal ligament. Thus, on trauma at the penile urethra, urine does not
extravasate into the anterior thigh.
The superficial perineal pouch is closed posteriorly by attaching the membranous layer of,
superficial fascia to the posterior margin of the urogenital diaphragm. Thus, commonly there is no
extravasation into the deep perineal pouch and the ischiorectal fossa.
Also, the membranous layer of superficial fascia attaches to the ischiopubic rami and forms the
lateral borders of the urogenital triangle. Thus, the urine does not extravasate to the thigh.
But, the ischiorectal fossa would be a better answer because of the following reasons:
+ Bulbourethral glands that lie in the deep perineal space open their ducts into the spongy urethra,
which is related to the superficial perineal space. Also, the muscles surrounding these glands were
recently found to be a continuation of the deep transverse perinei and tail of the external urethral
sphincter, both of which are components of the deep perineal space. Injury to the penile urethra
can damage bulbourethral glands’ ducts, which can lead to damage to the above-mentioned
structures and may cause extravasation of urine into the deep perineal space.
+ Previously, the perineal membrane was thought to be a triangular membrane, However, recent
studies show that it is contiguous anteriorly with the structures that support the urethra.
Posteriorly, it is related to the support of the perineal body and lateral vaginal wall. So damage to
the urethra might damage the anterior part of the perineal membrane which can make way for
extravasation of blood into the deep perineal space.
+ Deep perineal pouch: It was believed initially that the urogenital diaphragm was formed by 3
layers: An inferior fascia (also called the perineal membrane), deep perineal space, and a superior
fascia. The superior fascia formed the inferior border of the ischioanal fossa. The Colles fascia also
separates the perineal membrane from the ischioanal fossa. So, extravasation of blood into the
deep perineal space is possible but extravasation into the ischioanal fossa due to penile urethral
injury is highly unlikely. Recent studies have shown that this might be a slightly more complex
structure,

question: Which of the following muscles form the pelvic diaphragm?

explanation.detail:
Iliococeygeus and pubococeygeus are parts of the pelvic diaphragm, which is also known as the
levator ani.
The levator ani muscle is a complex unit that consists of 3 muscle components:
+ Pubocoecygeus
+ Pubovaginalis - elevates the urethra and hence contributes to urinary continence
+ Puboperinealis - draws the perineal body towards the pubic symphysis
+ Puboanalis - elevates the anus and keeps the urogenital hiatus narrowed
+ Puborectalis
+ Tiococeygeus
Levator ani muscles are the most important muscles in the pelvic floor and maintain a constant
state of contraction. They provide a stable floor and support the weight of the abdominopelvic
contents against intra-abdominal forces. Weakening of levator ani and pelvic fascia from
stretching or tearing during childbirth, may decrease support for the vagina, bladder, uterus, or
rectum or alter the position of the neck of the bladder and urethra.

The obturator internus forms the lateral wall of the ischioanal fossa and is not a part of the levator

The deep transverse perineal muscle is the content of the deep perineal space and contributes to

subject: Anatomy
question: Injury of the lower brachial plexus leads to Klumpke's paralysis. What is the characteristic clinical finding seen in this lesion?

explanation.detail:
The characteristic clinical finding in Klumpke's paralysis is a complete claw hand.
Klumpke's paralysis is caused by injury of the lower trunk of the brachial plexus-mainly Tt and
partially C8, due to traction injury of the lower trunk that may be seen in undue abduction of the
arm (outstretched arm grabbing an object during a fall from height) or birth injury (traction of the
extended arm during delivery)
This leads to paralysis of the intrinsic muscles of the hand (T1) that are responsible for flexion at
metacarpophalangeal joints (MCP) and extension at proximal and distal interphalangeal
joints(PIP and DIP). The unopposed action of extensor digitorum at MCP causes hyperextension.
The unopposed action of flexor digitorum superficialis and profundus at PIP and DIP causes
flexion. Thus giving rise to the characteristic claw hand.
Other changes include anesthesia and analgesia along the ulnar border of the forearm, vasomotor
changes, absence of sweating, and trophic changes in long-standing cases. If the C8 is also
damaged, the extent of anesthesia will be greater and will involve the medial side of the forearm,
hand, and medial two fingers.
Horner's syndrome (option D) may occur in one-third of the cases due to injury of sympathetic
fibers to the head and neck that leave the spinal cord through nerve T.

question: A 50-year-old male presented with a positive Trendelenburg test on the right side. Identify the nerve injured.

explanation.detail:
Positive Trendelenburg test on the right side indicates a right superior gluteal nerve injury.
When the patient stands on his right foot, there is drooping of the left pelvis, this is a Positive
Trendelenburg test on the right side.
The gluteus medius and minimus are powerful abductors of the hip supplied by the superior
gluteal nerve.
Normally, when the leg is lifted off the ground, the gluteus medius and minimus on the opposite
(unsupported) side exert traction on the hip bone. This results in a slight raising of the pelvis on
the unsupported side, which helps maintain the trunk upright position.
If the gluteus medius and minimus are paralyzed, the pelvis drops on the contralateral side. This
is known as a positive Trendelenburg sign. It is positive in the paralysis of the hip abductors, and
in developmental dysplasia of the hip. A characteristic lurching gait is seen.

question: A patient underwent surgery for the varicose vein. He now complains of sensory loss over the medial aspect of the leg and foot. Which of the following nerves is most likely to be injured?

explanation.detail:
The given clinical scenario in a patient with sensory loss over the medial aspect of the leg and foot
is suggestive of injury to the saphenous nerve. Injury to the saphenous nerve is common following
varicose vein surgery.
The saphenous nerve is the longest and largest cutaneous branch of the femoral nerve and is the
longest nerve in the body. It arises from the posterior division of the femoral nerve. It then enters
the adductor canal and exits it by piercing the fascia lata between the gracilis and sartorius and
supplies the skin over the patella. The saphenous nerve then descends along the medial border of
the tibia along with the great saphenous vein (anterior to the vein) and supplies the skin on the
medial side of the knee, leg, and foot up to the first metatarsophalangeal joint.
During stripping of veins in varicose surgery, injury to the saphenous nerve is common as it
closely travels with the great saphenous vein. Sensory loss over the medial side of the knee, leg,
and foot is observed in saphenous nerve injury.
Saphenous
Other options:
Option A: Sural nerve injury causes sensory loss over the posterior and lateral aspects of the distal
third of the leg and lateral side of the foot and fifth toe. It is often used as an autologous
peripheral nerve graft as it is readily identified and easy to harvest.
Option B: Superficial peroneal nerve injury causes sensory loss over the lateral aspect of the leg
and the dorsum of the foot. It also causes weakness of foot eversion.
Option C: Deep peroneal nerve injury causes weakness of ankle dorsiflexion and toe extension but
normal eversion of the foot.

Usually, in term infants, the ductus arteriosus closes within 72 hours of birth. Failure of the
ductus arteriosus to close results in patent ductus arteriosus (PDA).
PDA usually presents with a hyperdynamic precordium, bounding peripheral pulses, wide pulse
pressure, a machine-like continuous or systolic murmur, cardiomegaly, increased pulmonary
vascular markings, hepatomegaly, increasing oxygen dependence, carbon dioxide retention, and
renal failure.
Ina majority of cases, PDA closes spontaneously. If spontaneous closure fails to occur, COX
inhibitors like indomethacin or ibuprofen can be used. Surgical ligation is indicated in case of
contraindications to medical management or failure of the same.

but not PDA.

question: A patient presents with swelling under the left ear lobule and complains of pain. Which of the following structures is the likely cause of the pain?
options: Investing layer of deep cervical fascia

explanation.detail:
The cause of the pain in this patient complaining of pain in the parotid region is likely to be the
greater auricular nerve.
The greater auricular nerve provides sensory innervation to the skin overlying the parotid gland
and the ear lobule. Pain associated with swelling and inflammation in the parotid region could be
transmitted through the greater auricular nerve. The parotid gland is covered by a parotid capsule
that is derived from the investing layer of deep cervical fascia. Any acute inflammation of the
gland such as acute sialadenitis results in pain in the preauricular region. The pain is mainly due
to stretching of the capsule resulting in stimulation of the greater auricular nerve.
Other options:
Option A: The facial nerve is not the primary cause of pain in the parotid region due to swelling.
The Hitzelberger sign(numbness in the posterosuperior meatal wall of the external auditory
canal), associated with facial nerve involvement, is seen in acoustic neuroma and is not the cause
of pain in this scenario
Option D: Investing layer of deep cervical fascia is not the correct answer because the investing
layer of deep cervical fascia itself does not transmit pain sensation. ‘The investing layer of deep
cervical fascia is a layer of connective tissue that envelops the neck and extends to cover the
parotid gland, forming the parotid capsule. While inflammation or swelling in the parotid gland
can stretch the capsule and cause pain, the actual transmission of pain sensation is mediated by
sensory nerves, such as the greater auricular nerve, which innervates the skin over the parotid
gland and the ear lobule.
The greater auricular nerve (Option B) is a more appropriate answer in this context, as it is
responsible for carrying the pain sensation from the area of swelling and inflammation in the
parotid region.
Option C: The auriculotemporal nerve can cause referred pain due to caries tooth, ulcerative
lesions of the oral cavity, temporomandibular joint disorders, and sphenopalatine neuralgia.
However, it is not the primary cause of pain in the parotid region due to swelling.

question: Which of the following muscles form the pelvic diaphragm?

explanation.detail:
The levator ani muscle is a complex unit that consists of 3 muscle components:
+ Pubococcygeus
+ Pubovaginalis - elevates the urethra and hence contributes to urinary continence
+ Puboperinealis - draws the perineal body towards the pubic symphysis,
+ Puboanalis - elevates the anus and keeps the urogenital hiatus narrowed
+ Puborectalis,
+ Tliococeygeus
Levator ani muscles are the most important muscles in the pelvic floor and maintain a constant
state of contraction. They provide a stable floor and support the weight of the abdominopelvie
contents against intra-abdominal forces. Weakening of levator ani and pelvic fascia from
stretching or tearing during childbirth, may decrease support for the vagina, bladder, uterus, or
rectum or alter the position of the neck of the bladder and urethra.
Other options:
The obturator internus forms the lateral wall of the ischioanal fossa and is not a part of the levator
ani,
The deep transverse perineal muscle is the content of the deep perineal space and contributes to
the formation of the perineal body.

After the formation of the notochord, which is the axial structure, the mesoderm on either side
can be seen to be organized into three layers as follows:
1. Para-axial mesoderm - Thickened plate of the mesoderm close to the midline, on either side of
the notochord. This forms the somitomeres and somites, which have the following derivatives:
+ Dermatome: Forms the segmental dermis of the skin
+ Myotome: Forms the skeletal muscle
+ Sclerotome: Forms the vertebral column and ribs
2, Intermediate mesoderm - Longitudinal strip of mesoderm that connects the para-axial with the
lateral plate mesoderm. The urinary (Option A) and genital systems are derived from the
intermediate mesoderm.
3. Lateral plate mesoderm - A thinner layer of mesoderm that lies laterally. This layer develops a
large cavity called the intraembryonic coelom that gives rise to pericardial, pleural (Option C), and
peritoneal cavities.
The neural crest cells also contribute to the formation of structures as follows:
In the trunk region, they form melanocytes, sensory ganglia (Option B), sympathetic and enteric
neurons, Schwann cells, and adrenal medulla cells.
In the cranial region, they contribute to the craniofacial skeleton, neurons for cranial ganglia,
satellite glial cells, and melanocytes.

The surface of the cerebrum consists of folds called gyri separated by fissures called sulci. The
lateral sulcus, also called the Sylvian Fissure, is present on the infero-lateral surface of each
cerebral hemisphere and separates the frontal lobe from the temporal lobe. The lateral suleus
consists of a stem which divides into three rami, which are, the anterior horizontal, anterior
ascending, and the poster
rami.

The marked structure is the sub-aortic curtain. It is also called the trigonum fibrosum sinistrum.
The fibrous skeleton of the heart is formed by fibrous tissue that is continuous between the
atrio-ventricular and arterial orifices. They exist to keep the valves competent, allow attachment
of cardiac musculature, and act as an insulating layer for the conducting system of the heart. The
fibrous rings of importance are:
+ The atrioventricular fibrous rings - existing in a figure of 8 configuration, connecting the atria,
ventricles and the membranous part of the interventricular septum.
+ The trigonum fibrosum dextrum - between the atrioventricular rings and the aortie ring.
+ The trigonum fibrosum sinistrum - between the mitral valve and the aortic valve.

The arrow-marked structure in the given image is the terminal bronchiole. It can be identified by
the absence of cartilage, which would be present in the bronchus, and the presence of cilia and
stratified columnar epithelium, which would not be seen in respiratory bronchioles and alveolar
sac.
The following is a summary of the intrapulmonary passages:
Trachea > Main Bronchi + Lobar Bronchi > Segmental Bronchi + Terminal Bronchiole >
Respiratory Bronchiole + Alveolar Duct + Alveolar Sac > Alveoli
Bronchi and bronchioles can be distinguished by the presence of cartilage and glands in the
bronchi which are not present in the bronchioles.
Terminal bronchioles represent the most distal parts of the conducting passage. Each terminal
bronchiole ends by dividing into respiratory bronchioles. Terminal bronchioles contain cilia and
stratified columnar epithelium, whereas respiratory bronchioles contain few or no cilia, and
simple cuboidal epithelium. Another differentiating point between the two is that terminal
bronchioles would not communicate with the alveoli but respiratory bronchioles would have a
communication with the alveoli.
Each respiratory bronchiole ends by dividing into a few alveolar ducts. Each alveolar duet ends in
a passage, the atrium, which leads into a number of rounded alveolar sacs. Each alveolar sac is
studded with a number of air sacs or alveoli.
A; Terminal Bronchiole, B: Respiratory Bronchiole, C: Alveolar Sac, D: Alveolar Duct

A patient with dropping of the right hip on lifting the right foot off the ground, has a positive
Trendelenburg's sign. The sciatic nerve would not be involved.
A positive Trendelenburg's sign is seen in the paralysis of the gluteus medius and gluteus minimus
muscles, both of which are supplied by the superior gluteal nerve. In the scenario mentioned,
since the patient has dropping of the hip on the right side, the structures on the left side would be
affected.
Mechanism of actions of gluteus medius and minimus:
+ When the leg on one side is lifted off the ground, making it the unsupported side, gluteus
medius and minimus muscles on the opposite side, which is the supported side, exert traction on
the hip bone by acting from the femur. This action results in slight raising of the pelvis on the
unsupported side, which helps maintain the trunk in the upright position.
+ When the gluteus medius and minimus are paralyzed, the pelvis on the unsupported side sinks
when the patient lifts the foot off the ground, This causes the patients to have a characteristic
lurching gait.

streak and invaginate into the primitive pit and organizes itself into cells arranged in three layers,
which eventually develop into ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm. The prechordal plate simply
refers to the cells that have invaginated in the cranial region.

The marked structure is the subthalamic nucleus, which is a part of the basal ganglia and is
concerned with motor function.
The basal ganglia refers to the mass of grey matter located within the cerebral white matter and is
responsible for a number of coordinated functions including the control of extra-pyramidal
pathways for motor coordination. There are two major pathways involved which are, the direct
and indirect pathways.
The direct pathway stimulates the cortex to produce movement, whereas the indirect pathway
inhibits it. The striatal activity in the direct pathway is mediated by D1 dopamine receptors and in
the indirect pathway by D2 dopamine receptors. The subthalamic nucleus is a part of the indirect
pathway. A lesion at subthalamic nuclei leads to loss of inhibitory effect of the indirect pathway
resulting in dopaminergic overactivity responsible for features of hemiballismus.

subject: Anatomy
question: On neurologic examination, a patient is not able to identify an object but is able to recognize the object from its texture, colour and pattern. Which of the following is likely to have been affected?

explanation.detail:
The clinical scenario suggests that the patient has visual agnosia and the likely site of involvement
would be the inferior temporal lobe.
+ Agnosia refers to the inability to visually identify objects and is also called optic aphasia
According to De Jong, visual agnosia is further classified into:
+ Apperceptive visual agnosia - the visual image appears distorted to the patient and hence they
are unable to recognise it. This occurs due lesions in the parieto-oceipital region
+ Associative visual agnosia - inability to visually identify objects in the absence of visual
impairment, with ability to identify the object using other sensory modalities (as suggested in the
scenario). This occurs due to lesions of occipito-temporal junction, which is present in the inferior
temporal lobe. Prosopagnosia is a type of associatetive visual agnosia which refers to the
inability to visually identify faces.
The occipital lobe acts as the primary visual area and a lesion in this region would result in
cortical blindness and achromatopsia, which is the inability to visualise colour. In the given
scenario, as the patient is also able to identify colour, it indicates that the occipital lobe (Option A)
is unaffected and (hence inferior temporal lobe is the better answer).
Stereognosis is the ability to identify an object by a perception of the form and nature of an object
without looking at it. In the given scenario, the patient is able to recognize the object from its
texture and pattern which indicates that stereognosis is intact. Loss of this ability is known as
astereognosis which occurs in lesions of the superior parietal lobule (Options B and C) and dorsal
column pathways.

Apocrine glands are present in the axilla.
+ Apocrine
+ Merocrine
+ Holocrine
In apocrine glands, the apical parts of the cells are shed off to discharge the secretion. Examples
of apocrine glands are :
+ Secretion of milk fat by mammary glands
+ Atypical sweat glands in the axilla, perianal region, periumbilical skin, mons pubis.
+ Ceruminous glands of the external auditory meatus are modified apocrine glands
Apocrine glands are large glands found in the dermis or subcutaneous fat. They are a type of
sweat gland. It consists of a basal secretory coil followed by a straight duet that opens into
the pilary canal above the duct of the sebaceous gland. In hairless skin, it directly opens onto the
skin surface.
Apocrine activity is minimal before puberty, after which it is androgen-dependent and responsive
to emotional stimuli. It is controlled by adrenergic nerves and is sensitive to adrenaline and
noradrenaline. The secretion is initially sterile and odorless, but it undergoes bacterial
decomposition to generate odorous and musky compounds. Obstruction of apocrine sweat ducts
and associated upper hair follicles in the axillae, breast areolae, and pubic region, mainly in
women, is thought to cause Fox-Fordyce disease. It is also implicated in the causation of a
painful, occasionally debilitating condition called hidradenitis suppurativa.

subject: Anatomy
question: The spinal sensory nucleus of the trigeminal nerve has 2nd order neurons to carry which sensation?

explanation.detail:
The trigeminal nerve (cranial nerve V) is responsible for sensory input from the face, as well as,
motor functions such as chewing. Its sensory pathway involves three branches: the ophthalmic
(V1), maxillary (V2), and mandibular (V3) divisions. The first-order neurons for each division
originate from different sensory ganglia:
1.Ophthalmic Division (V1):
First-order neurons: Cell bodies are located in the trigeminal ganglion (also called the semilunar
or Gasserian ganglion).
2. Maxillary Division (V2):
First-order neurons: Cell bodies are located in the trigeminal ganglion.
3. Mandibular Division (V3):
First-order neurons: Cell bodies are located in the trigeminal ganglion.
1. Principal Sensory Nucleus (Main Sensory Nucleus):
a.Receives input from all three divisions of the trigeminal nerve.
b.Located in the pons.
c.Second-order neurons here carry discriminative touch and proprioceptive information.
2, Spinal Trigeminal Nucleus (Spinal Nucleus of Trigeminal):
a.Receives input mainly from the maxillary and mandibular divisions,
b.Located in the medulla.
c.Second-order neurons here carry pain and temperature sensations.
After synapsing in these nuclei, the sensory information is further relayed to higher brain centers
for processing and perception.

The given specimen is that of an epiphyseal cartilage, where zone 3 corresponds to the zone of
calcification.
Epiphyseal cartilage is divided into five zones :
+ The resting zone consists of hyaline cartilage without morphological changes in the cells (option
a)
+ In the proliferative zone, chondrocytes divide rapidly and form columns of stacked cells parallel
to the long axis of the bone (option 2)
+ The hypertrophic cartilage zone contains large chondrocytes whose cytoplasm has accumulated
glycogen. The resorbed matrix is reduced to thin septa between the chondrocytes.
+ Simultaneous with the death of chondrocytes in the calcified cartilage zone, the thin septa of
cartilage matrix become calcified by the deposit of hydroxyapatite.
+ In the ossification zone, endochondral bone tissue appears (option 4). Blood capillaries and
osteoprogenitor cells formed by mitosis of cells originating from the periosteum invade the
cavities left by the chondrocytes. The osteoprogenitor cells form osteoblasts, which are distributed
in a discontinuous layer over the septa of the calcified cartilage matrix. Ultimately, the osteoblasts
deposit bone matrix over the three-dimensional caleified cartilage matrix.
The epiphyseal cartilage is responsible for the growth in the length of bone and it disappears in
adults, which is why bone growth ceases in adulthood.

The ischial spine is a bony prominence found on the ischium, one of the three bones that make up

pelvic plane which comprises of :

+ When the fetal head station is at ‘0’, the head is considered to be engaged.
+ Deep transverse arrest occurs at this plane.
+ The ischial spine can serve as a landmark in pudendal anaesthesia, as the pudendal nerve lies
close to the ischial spine

subject: Anatomy
question: Which of the following statements is true concerning the anatomy of the breast?
options: Inner quadrants contain most breast tissue
Ligaments of cooper are found only in lower quadrants
Tail of Spence extends across anterior axillary fold

explanation.detail:
The tail of Spence extends across the anterior axillary fold and is a small extension of the upper
lateral quadrant of the breast.
The breast lies in the superficial fascia of the pectoral region. It is divided into four quadrants, i.e.
upper medial, upper lateral, lower medial and lower lateral.
Vertically, it extends from the second to the sixth ribs. Horizontally, it extends from the lateral
border of the sternum to the midaxillary line. A small extension of the upper lateral quadrant,
called the axillary tail of Spence, passes through an opening in the deep fascia and lies in the
axilla. The opening is called the foramen of Langer.

The breasts normally comprise up to 20 discrete lobes that contain ductal-glandular tissue
consisting of branching ducts and terminal secretory lobules in a connective tissue stroma. Each
lobe is derived by branching from one central lactiferous duct in the nipple. The terminal duct
lobular unit (TDLU or lobule) is the functional milk secretory component of the breast.
The upper outer quadrant of the breast contains a greater volume of tissue than the other
quadrants. This is the reason for the majority of the carcinomas arising in the upper outer

The CT finding in the image is suggestive of an aberrant right subclavian artery.
An aberrant right subclavian artery occurs when the right fourth aortic arch and right dorsal aorta
involute cranial to the seventh intersegmental artery.
This artery arises distal to the left subclavian artery and crosses the midline posterior to the
esophagus. Because of the absence of a normal right subclavian-carotid junction to pull down the
right recurrent laryngeal nerve (RLN) during embryologic development, the right RLN follows a
straight path from the vagus to the larynx and is called a non-recurrent right laryngeal nerve.
Most patients with this anomaly are asymptomatic, and the aberrant subclavian artery is of no
clinical consequence. Dysphagia caused by compression of the esophagus by an abnormal
subclavian artery is called dysphagia lusoria.

+ Stem of the lateral sulcus, also called the Sylvian fissure. It houses the middle cerebral artery and
its branches, playing a crucial role in brain vascularization and cortical organization.
During surgeries, the pterion is a common site for trephining (making a hole in the skull).

subject: Anatomy
question: A 60-year-old male recovering from facial nerve palsy complains of food getting stuck between the cheek and teeth while eating. What is the muscle affected in this case?

explanation.detail:
This patient, recovering from facial nerve palsy, getting food stuck between the cheek and teeth
while eating, is because of the buccinator being affected.
The buccinator is a muscle of facial expression. It is innervated by the facial nerve. It is known as
the whistling muscle.
The buccinator muscle is primarily responsible for keeping food between the teeth during
mastication by pressing the cheek against the teeth (involved in flattening the cheeks against the
gums and teeth).
In facial nerve palsy, paralysis of the buccinator muscle can lead to food accumulating in the
cheek pouch, as the muscle is unable to contract properly.
Other options:
Option B: Levator Labii Superioris: This muscle elevates the upper lip and contributes to facial
expressions like showing the upper teeth and dilates the nostrils. It is not involved in keeping food
in place during chewing.
Option C : Zygomaticus Major: Responsible for pulling the corner of the mouth upward and
outward, contributing to smiling. It does not influence food placement during mastication,
Option D : Orbicularis Oris: Encircles the mouth and helps in closing the lips. While it is involved
in mastication and speech, its paralysis would not cause food to accumulate in the cheek.

subject: Anatomy
question: Which of the following is not a derivative of neural crest cells?

explanation.detail:
Retinal pigment epithelium is not a derivative of neural crest cells.
Retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) is derived from the neuroectoderm. The RPE develops from the
outer layer of the optic cup, a structure that arises from the neural tube (neuroectodermal origin),
not from neural crest cells.
Neural crest cells are a group of cells that migrate during embryonic development and give rise to
various structures, including parts of the nervous system, face, and heart.
Neural crest cells (NCC) are present adjacent to the neural tube and are sometimes referred to as
the fourth germ layer. It contributes to the formation of many organs and tissues:
In the trunk region, they form:
+ Melanocytes (Option D)
+ Sensory ganglia
+ Sympathetic and enteric neurons
In the cranial region, they contribute to the:
+ Craniofacial skeleton
+ Neurons for cranial ganglia-Ciliary ganglion (Option C)
+ Ciliary muscles (Option A)
+ Satellite glial cells,
+ Melanocytes
Some of the other neural crest derivatives are:
+ C cells of the thyroid gland
+ Conotruncal septum in the heart
+ Odontoblasts
+ Smooth muscle cells to blood vessels to the face and forebrain.

question: The cranial component of the neural crest cells leads to the formation of?

explanation.detail:
The cranial component of neural crest cells (NCC) leads to the formation of Conotruncal septum.
The conotruncal septum is also known as the aortopulmonary septum. The formation of the
aortopulmonary septum in the truncus arteriosus divides the outflow channel of the heart into the
ventral aorta and the pulmonary trunk. Persistent truncus arteriosus results when the conotruncal
ridges fail to form.
The development of the heart begins in the third week of embryogenesis with the formation of the
cardiogenic mesoderm. The heart tube forms through fusion of paired endocardial tubes, which
then undergo looping and septation to establish the four chambers. By the end of the eighth week,
the heart achieves its definitive structure, with separate atria, ventricles, and major vessels.
The heart develops from mesodermal origins, with different parts deriving from specific
embryonic structures:
+ First Heart Field (Primary Heart Field): Forms the left ventricle and part of the atria.
+ Second Heart Field: Contributes to the right ventricle, outflow tract (conus arteriosus and
truncus arteriosus), and part of the atria.
+ Neural Crest Cells: Contribute to the septation of the outflow tract and the formation of the
aorticopulmonary septum.
+ Proepicardium (Epicardial Cells): Forms the epicardium and contributes to coronary vessels.
+ Sinus Venosus: Forms part of the right atrium (sinus venarum) and the coronary sinus.
+ Endocardial Cushions: Form the atrioventricular valves and contribute to septation.

subject: Anatomy
question: Which of the following is the first to be formed by the migration of the epiblast along the primitive streak?

explanation.detail:
Endoderm is the first to be formed by the migration of the epiblast along the primitive streak.
Gastrulation is when the single-layered blastula reorganizes into a multi-layered structure called
the gastrula. This process establishes the three primary germ layers that give rise to all tissues and
organs of the body. During gastrulation, epiblast cells migrate through the primitive streak to
form the three germ layers: endoderm, mesoderm, and ectoderm.
Cells from the epiblast migrate inward (invagination) through the primitive streak
The first group of migrating epiblast cells replaces the hypoblast to form the definitive endoderm.
The endoderm gives rise to structures like the gastrointestinal tract, liver, and lungs.
Subsequent migrating cells spread between the epiblast and the newly formed endoderm to create
the mesoderm, which forms structures like muscles, bones, and the cardiovascular system.
Remaining epiblast cells that do not migrate through the primitive streak form the ectoderm,
which gives rise to the epidermis and the nervous system.

Option D: Neural Groove: The neural groove is part of the ectoderm and forms during

question: During walking, the hip bone on the side of the suspended leg is raised by the action of which muscle present in the supporting leg?

explanation.detail:
Actions of Gluteus Medius:
+ Hip abduction
+ Stabilizes the pelvis during gait.
+ Assists the internal rotation of the thigh
Gluteus medius and gluteus minimus act as the abductors of the hip joint. Stabilizing the pelvis on
the femur during walking is done by these muscles. It is demonstrated by asking the subject to
stand on one limb.
Trendelenburg Sign:
Trendelenburg sign is a clinical sign used to assess the function of the gluteus medius.
Its positive when the pelvis drops on the opposite side of the stance leg, indicating weakness or
dysfunction of the gluteus medius on the supporting leg.
Ina healthy individual, the gluteus medius contracts to prevent the pelvis from tilting when one
leg is lifted. If the gluteus medius is weak or paralyzed, the pelvis tilts toward the side of the
suspended leg.
Option B: Gluteus Maximus: Extends and externally rotates the hip; key for climbing stairs and
rising from sitting. Weakness causes difficulty in hip extension, affecting activities like standing
up or climbing.
Option C: Obturator Internus: Externally rotates and stabilizes the hip joint; abduets the thigh
when the hip is flexed. Dysfunction can impair hip rotation, commonly affected in pelvic injuries.
Option D: Quadratus Femoris: Externally rotates and adduets the hip. Injury or entrapment of the
nerve to quadratus femoris can cause deep hip pain and weakness in rotation.
